# ODS design part 1: Bare minimum collaboration

**Author(s):** [Jan-Jan van der Vyver](mailto:jan-jan@parity.io)  
**Status:** In review  
**Created:** 2026-05-03  
**Last Updated:** 2026-05-11  
**LGTMs**: \<add your name here /\> 

# Overview

The simplest/fastest way to create collaborative software that provides organizational data sovereignty, is to extend the existing subset of local-first software that is aligned with the Web3 ethos, especially local-first access-control, by introducing the concept of an organisation. We define organisation members and devices off-chain to keep them private (in a sparse Merkle trie), but we anchor the root hash on-chain to create a shared verifiable state/truth. This approach reduces the inherited local-first attack surface (i.e. disabling time based and identity takeover attacks), and will be useful in future on-chain role-based smart contract functionality. Additionally we add an organisation wide public key on-chain, to simplify access control at the organisation level and to scale to large organisations. 

# Context & Goals

* **Background:**   
  * Organisational data sovereignty is a priority for many organisations, especially in the current political circumstance. See the [PRD](?tab=t.0) for details.   
  * Recent advances in blockchain and local-first software has created an opportunity to create collaboration solutions for organisations that are built with true data sovereignty as the unique selling proposition.  
* **Goals:**   
  * Create a library for defining organisation members (and their devices) trie.  
    * With the ability to add/delete/update members/devices.  
  * Create access control library for data objects that respect data sovereignty (by extending local-first access control), that:   
    * Delegate access control rights at a member level, or at the organisation level.  
    * Is robust against network delays and people working offline.  
    * Enables organisation members to identify/verify other organization members/devices in good standing.  
    * Is capable of creating/updating/rotating shared secrets used to communicate the CRDTs used in local-first collaboration.  
  * Enable access control that suffices for the use cases “[1.a: Create, collaborate, share](?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.b06j9ndon3jo)” and “[1.b: Meeting notes](?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.5p2ws9p6j8pa)”.  
* **Non-Goals:**  
  * Creating novel cryptography methodologies/algorithms/code.  
  * Collaboration with members of other organisations or individuals.  
  * Things that local-first software cover e.g.:  
    * Collaborator device discovery.  
    * Establishing & securing peer connections.  
    * Peer-to-peer syncing.  
  * Communicating between admins to determine new organisation members trie.  
  * Data object (which is comprised of CRDTs):  
    * Definition.  
    * Discovery and search.  
    * Backup.  
    * Sharing via relay servers.  
  * Implementing the local-first collaboration software itself.  
  * Organisation discovery.

# Proposed Design

The subset of local-first software that is aligned with the Web3 ethos is data sovereign by design and has collaborative capabilities, but existing access control functionality/designs are limited to individuals (and their devices) and are vulnerable to time-based attacks. By linking local-first access control to a blockchain mediated definition of organisation membership, those attack vectors can be protected against and we can benefit from all future advancements in related local-first software/functionality. 

Furthermore, this would enable a best-of-both-worlds approach, where the same membership definition, especially if supplemented with organisational roles, could also be used to determine access for the kind of work not-suited for local-first collaboration, i.e. those ideal for on-chain collaboration. But, this will be addressed in future designs.

## Core design pieces {#core-design-pieces}

![][image1]

1. Members of an organisation are defined off-chain in a sparse Merkle trie:  
   1. Keeping the data off-chain keeps private who the members of the organisation are (specifically from non-members in this simple implementation where the full members trie is shared amongst members, but as per next point, in future even within organisation privacy is possible).  
   2. Note: The sparse Merkle trie simplifies creating zero knowledge proofs of membership, which is needed for a future design.  
2. Each member leaf node includes a sub-trie identifying their devices:  
   1. It is optimised for 2 devices per member and future zero-knowledge proofs.  
   2. Defining individual devices also allows per device revocation (in addition to per member revocation).  
3. Thus a 1:1 mapping is created between a member leaf (in the members trie) and member-as-a-group in the local-first software, with their devices as group members. Note:  
   1. Changes to keys in the members trie need to be “immediately” reflected (i.e. deterministically reflected as the member trie update propagates) in the local-first access control, i.e. member-as-a-group.  
   2. Delegating of access control rights within the local-first software must be constrained to groups, i.e. delegating rights to a device needs to be disabled.  
   3. Peer discovery and peer-to-peer authentication happen at the device level (as per current local-first best practices):   
      1. In this simple design all organisation members have access to each member’s public key along with their per device public keys (via the members trie), and these public keys can be used as the basis for authentication — the counterparty must hold the corresponding secret key.  
      2. This will also be the encryption used for establishing the initial direct communication between these peers and communication that does not relate to specific data objects. At the data object level the local-first access control will determine the in-transit encryption key.  
   4. Simply put the device keys are used to determine whether a peer-to-peer connection can be established, and the member-as-a-group keys are used to determine whether changes to a data object will be accepted (along with what the in-transit encryption should be, see next point).  
4. To preserve forward secrecy (at the cost of relay nodes) data-object encryption at-rest differ from in-transit:  
   1. At-rest: Data objects at-rest are encrypted with a secret key specific to the device.   
      1. This is common across all data objects (associated with the organisation).   
      2. This is expected to seldomly or even never change.   
      3. Prevents thrashing upon changes for root hash (i.e. no need to re-encrypt data objects).  
   2. In-transit: Data objects in-transit are encrypted with a secret key derived by the local-first access control, resulting in forward secrecy and post-compromise security. Using the local-first library’s Continuous Group Key Agreement (CGKA) mechanism.  
      1. I.e. if the public key of a member, who is also a direct delegate in data object’s local-first access control, changed, then the shared secret used for in-transit encryption needs to be changed/recalculated.  
5. The organisation also has a public and secret key pair, so that (along with the organisation identifier) access control can also be delegated/assigned at an organisation-as-a-pseudo-group level (as per the requirements of the “[1.a: Create, collaborate, share](?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.b06j9ndon3jo)” and “[1.b: Meeting notes](?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.5p2ws9p6j8pa)” examples from the PRD).  
   1. Because the organisation key pair is calculated / agreed to by a small set of admins, instead of the whole organisation, it is O(1) regardless of organisation size.  
   2. Updating the in-transit secret with CGKA for data object with access granted to the organisation-as-a-pseudo-group works basically exactly the same as for member-as-a-group, except that it suffice for any single organisation member to participate since they all have access to the organisation secret key (instead of requiring them all to participate), i.e. improved scaling.  
   3. NB. Though the access is granted at the organisational level, whether individual writes are accepted is still checked at the member resolution (by falling back on the members trie). This is a key extension to existing local-first software functionality.  
6. To communicate changes (e.g. members’ device keys that have been revoked or/and memberships that have been revoked or added) the bare minimum information is created/updated on-chain, which then triggers peer-to-peer distribution of the change details:  
   1. A threshold of admins is required to update the on-chain information.   
      1. The threshold protects against a below threshold number of rogue admins.  
      2. For this simple design we will use a standard Polkadot proxy multisig, BUT that brings with it two disadvantages (which will be addressed in future designs):  
         1. It violates data sovereignty, because the public keys of the organisation admins are known / can be identified.  
         2. It requires organisation admins to be managed separately from the members trie.  
   2. The on-chain update actually contains 3 pieces of information that are all updated together:   
      1. The latest trie root hash.  
      2. The latest organisation public key.   
      3. A monotonically increasing epoch.  
   3. The triggered peer-to-peer distribution (starting from the admins who signed for the on-chain update) contains:  
      1. The corresponding members trie delta.  
      2. The corresponding latest organisation secret key.  
   4. The on-chain information is used to verify what is distributed peer-to-peer.  
7. Locally adding/updating/revoking membership (notification via on-chain root hash change):  
   1. Members will request from peers/admins (1) a members trie delta and (2) a new organisation-wide secret key, which they then verify against the on-chain information.  
      1. A member that has lost their status will not be given the new organisation-wide secret key, i.e. they will be excluded from all communication (since it will be derived from the new organisation key pair).  
   2. Revocation: Should a member (or just one of their devices) be deleted the software will locally delete all their copies of the organization’s data objects (across all their affected devices).  
   3. Remaining members recalculate their access control secret for affected data objects using CGKA.  
8. Locally adding/updating/revoking a member’s device(s) triggered via on-chain root hash change:  
   1. Same steps a and b as for adding/updating/removing a member in the point above.  
   2. All connections to/from removed devices will be cancelled/refused.  
   3. There is only need for per-data-object access control secret rotation (via CGKA) if the member’s key changed, because the member’s key is not derived from the member’s per device keys, so e.g. adding a device does not require rotation. However, when a device is removed, the group key should be rotated (because that device had access to the member’s secret key), which then triggers per-data-object key rotation in any data object where the member is granted access.  
9. To reduce local thrashing on access control recalculations due to on-chain member changes, the approach is to:  
   1. first update/recalculate the CGKA, for use in in-transit encryption, of a data object actively being collaborated on (to reduce perceptible latency),  
   2. then update the CGKA of recently opened data objects, and   
   3. finally, do not bother to update the CGKA of the rest of the data objects (unless they get shared again, because the CGKA is only relevant for in-transit encryption).  
10. Rejecting changes by ex-members to data objects in a distributed system (under the constraints that CRDTs cannot be deleted to maintain the convergence properties of local-first software, but only reverse operations applied):  
    1. Transitive trust (i.e. the initial simple version) — acceptance of all changes that other members accepted, i.e. till the time those members learned of ex-member or ex-device.  
       1. This is a perfectly reasonable approach/solution under the assumptions that   
          1. member devices will be mostly online,   
          2. the on-chain values are checked regularly, and   
          3. membership/device changes are communicated and acted upon quickly.  
       2. The 2nd and 3rd assumption can/will be helped along by if a member device learns of an on-chain change it “immediately” informs all the other member devices it is actively collaborating with. And similarly for disseminating the off-chain changes.  
       3. The 3rd assumption could be removed, if as soon as a device learns of an on-chain change, it pauses all collaboration/communication and only re-instates those that are still valid under the new members trie.  
       4. The assumption that member devices will be mostly online while reasonable in the modern world isn’t necessarily true, in fact, local-first software specifically allows members to collaborate peer-to-peer on a local network without the ability to check for on-chain updates (i.e. be able to verify that both parties are still members in good standing). This can be compensated for by an organisation policy that disables collaboration if the last on-chain check was too long ago (and potentially a similar enactment for revocation but with a more permissive time window).  
    2. \[future-scope\] Removing the taint (i.e. a more correct/robust version): applying reverse operations to all tainted operations received in a window 𝝉:   
       1. This functionality would be required for organisations that specifically foresee members being offline for protracted periods of time.  
       2. In essence there exists a window 𝝉 between the last time the member’s device checked the on-chain values and now.  
       3. If during 𝝉 there has been revocations of members or their devices, then there potentially exists operations received from the ex-members/devices during 𝝉, i.e. tainted operations. This requires mechanisms to:   
          1. identify tainted operations, and  
          2. reverse said tainted operations.  
       4. Reversing CRDTs operations require a per CRDT-type specific reversal.  
       5. This is considered out-of-scope for this design due to the complexity, but will be addressed in future work.

## Local-first libraries: Ink & Switch (Keyhive) vs p2panda:

As far as holding true to the Web3 ethos in discovery, auth, syncing, etc. there are only really two contenders that “does it all” in the local-first space — [Ink & Switch](https://www.inkandswitch.com/local-first-software/) and [p2panda](http://p2panda.org). It should be possible to swap out either of these libraries in future, but the initial implementation will be with libraries that are simplest to integrate. 

### Key changes required {#key-changes-required}

The majority of the local-first libraries should be usable as is, but some of it needs to be replaced with new functionality:

1. Introduce stable identities: Local-first ACLs delegate privileges to raw public keys, whereas we want it delegated to stable identities instead (i.e. a member trie leaf id or organisation id) i.e. replacing the direct use of the key in the ACL with a trie lookup for the correct key to use. This makes key rotation robust against identity takeover attacks (i.e. attacker learns keys of a device and user, then rotates that user out of all the corresponding access-control definitions). Note, however, this is a significant change to key rotation.  
2. Organisation-as-a-pseudo-group: This has no analog in local-first. The "any single member can read/write under it" semantics — especially with the per-device-signature write verification falling back to the trie — requires implementing a new principal type that neither library supports natively.  
3. The sole sources of truth for members and their devices public keys are the members trie and the organisation public key is the on-chain entry:   
   1. The members trie is the sole write authority for member/device key state, i.e. it is the *only* mechanism that can (1) change the set of public keys associated with said member’s devices, or (2) rotate the member's public key. Similarly for the on-chain organisation public key. Any library-native operation that would otherwise mutate this state (Keyhive's group delegations, p2panda-auth's add/remove member ops) is structurally disabled.  
   2. Library queries about member/device keys will be routed through trie lookups, instead of the library's internal state.   
4. CGKA triggers:  
   1. CGKA itself doesn’t change, but can now also be triggered by a key rotation on the members trie if it affected   
      1. the member-as-a-group public key (i.e. on device removal) or   
      2. the organisation-as-a-pseudo-group public key (i.e. every on-chain update).  
   2. If the CGKA is triggered it will now read the required keys from the members trie instead (because the access control now links to stable identities instead of keys).  
5. Peer-to-peer connections: Peer-to-peer libraries should remain unchanged at the protocol level, but the policy layer   
   1. now consults the members trie to decide whether to establish a connection, or  
   2. is triggered by changes in the members trie to assess whether it should maintain/drop an existing connection.

# Alternatives Considered

* **Membership secrecy**:  
  * Capturing the organisation members trie on-chain, would both be wasteful in terms of space used, and violate data sovereignty by revealing all the organisation members to the public.  
  * Hiding the complete set of members from other members, would require zero-knowledge proofs; this will be addressed in future designs.  
* **Threshold of admins**: The proxy multisig for updating the on-chain root hash was chosen to keep things as simple as possible. Future work could and should replace it with an offline threshold signature (e.g. Flexible Round-Optimized Schnorr Threshold) in combination with a non-interactive zero-knowledge proof to not violate data sovereignty (i.e. reveal admins) and to have a single source-of-truth for organisation membership.  
* **Relationship between members trie leaves, and local-first member-as-a-group (with devices)**:   
  * **Merkle trie leaves equal members (excluding devices)**: This is the simplest implementation, where a members trie leaf only captures the member, including the group public key of their local-first group that contains their devices. The member themselves is solely responsible for adding/removing/updating their devices (i.e. their per device keys). This provides a clean simple link/interface between the members trie and the local-first member-as-a-group (with devices), BUT the revocation of a device is then vulnerable to the time based attacks of local-first only access control. This option was deemed unacceptable due to the vulnerability to time based attacks.   
  * **Merkle trie leaves equal members & secondary on-chain list of revoked devices**: This combines the "Merkle trie leaves equal members” with an on-chain list of revoked devices. This does restore the protection against time based attacks, at the cost of leaking data online about who the members of an organisation are, because they are the only ones who must sign / pay for transactions to revoke devices. This leaking of membership data violates the data sovereignty requirement.  
  * **Merkle trie leaves equal member’s devices**: This approach has the per device revocation guarantees (i.e. the removal of the vulnerability to back-dated time attacks per device), but comes at the cost of :  
    * A bigger member Merkle trie (because each member is expected to have 1 or more devices), and a more complex mapping between the trie and local-first person-as-a-group that needs to be kept in sync.  
    * There is no single node defining a member (handle, public keys, smart contract roles), instead it is across multiple nodes that need to be kept in check/sync.  
  * **Merkle trie leaves equal member and their devices**: This approach (which is the approach chosen) creates a leaf identifying a member, with a sub-trie for their devices. This maps 1:1 to the local-first member-as-a-group, and protects against time-based attacks when removing devices or members.  
    * The synchronisation contract is that trie operations on the leaf drive changes in the local-first libraries (as described in “[Core design pieces](#core-design-pieces)” section).  
    * Comes at the cost of more complex device addition/update flows compared to per-member-only leaves, because the trie operation must be coordinated with the / reflected in a local-first group operation. This complexity is judged acceptable given the time-attack protection it provides.  
    * It also enables zero-knowledge proofs of (handle, role) over the members trie leaf without exposing a member’s other devices in the proof.

# Cross-Cutting Concerns

* **Decentralization:** This solution is 100% decentralized. Point of concern (though out of scope) could be:  
  * **Data-backup**: Naively, all data that a user has access to would be replicated across all their devices.  
    * If they have only 1 device, theft would result in complete loss of data that only they have access to, or have not shared since they have been offline. This is presumed to be ok, since truly valuable data will be shared in the organisation.  
  * **Storage requirements**: As the organisation grows in size and age, the number of data objects will accumulate, since all data objects a member has access to are replicated across all their devices. This will likely require consideration in future designs, especially for organisations with hundreds of thousands of members.  
* **Privacy**:  
  * Data objects and the organisation members trie are local to members’ devices. Encrypted both at rest (with key specific to the user device) and in transit (with a key specific to the group that have access to the data).   
  * Who are the members of an organisation is “leaked”/known to all members of an organisation, BUT not to those without.  
  * No metadata is leaked in this design about who is collaborating with whom, nor on what, in this design. (That said the peer discovery mechanism used by the local-first software might, but that should be addressed there.)   
  * The only leaked metadata is the public keys of the admins signing the on-chain root hash update.  
* **Scalability:**  
  * This access control should scale fine for small to medium organisations (up to 1000s of members).  
    * Peer-to-peer distribution of changes in organisation members trie.  
    * BeeKEM updates are O(log N) where N is the number of leaves in a data object's tree. For documents where access is granted to the organisation pseudo-member, N remains small (typically the document's named writers plus the org leaf), independent of total org size. This allows scaling to organisations of thousands of members without per-document trees becoming large.  
    * To reduce perceived latency of updating the secret keys of all data objects (i.e. minimise the SKU window), choosing which data objects secret keys to update first should be done smartly, i.e. first data objects being actively collaborated on, then data objects recently collaborated on, and there might be value in not even bothering updating the secret keys of older data objects (i.e. data objects that have not been read or edited in a long while).   
  * If a member is removed from an organization the access control for all the data objects they were explicitly specified in needs to be updated. This is O(m \* log n) where m \= number of data objects with the ex-member is a leaf node, and n \= number of remaining leaves in the data object’s BeeKEM. This can happen in the background, because the following two steps are immediate:  
    * The ex-member’s devices delete all company related data objects.  
    * Existing members refuse to establish/maintain connections with the ex-member.  
  * The other scaling problems expected is inherent in local-first software:  
    * Peer-to-peer sync of data object updates.  
      * This will likely become problematic if 100s of individuals try to edit the same data object at the same time. However, this is an edge case.  
    * The caveat is if an organisation has a large amount of data objects shared company wide:  
      * This could make the initial load of data objects for a new member take a long time. This likely needs investigation in future to do smarter sync-ing on the local-first sync side.  
      * This might for extremely large organisations increase the device storage requirements.  
* **Latency:**   
  * Local-first software is robust against being offline, and all delays can be translated into others being offline (for short periods).  
  * Local-first software also makes all edits local, so latency should be imperceptible (assuming the device has the compute to process the CRDTs timeously).  
  * The key delays are in how long it takes to:  
    * Update the root hash on-chain. This should be invisible to the members, and as such of no concern.  
    * Get the add/deleted/amended members/devices  
* **Security:**  
  * Data is envisaged to be encrypted at rest and in transit (these libraries will only determine the respective keys used).  
  * Ideally the implementation should use the device’s secure hardware module for key storage.  
  * Because the organisation secret key is shared amongst all members, it would grant an attacker access to all data objects shared at the organisation level, but that is the same as compromising a member's keys. So this isn’t an increased risk. In fact the organisation keys will be rotated a lot more often then member’s keys.  
  * To strengthen the [transitive trust assumptions](#bookmark=id.8yt8biv4q2z6), the design includes a:  
    * An optional organisation policy that disables collaboration if the last on-chain check exceeds an organisation set time window (to prevent ex-members from maliciously collaborating offline).  
    * An optional organisation policy that deletes all local organisation data if the member device has been offline exceeding an organisation set time window (to prevent ex-members from keeping organisation data forever by air gapping one of their devices).  
    * An optional organisation policy to distrust all other member devices (i.e. treat them as “offline”) as soon as a device learns of an on-chain change, and then to only reestablish connections if justified by the updated members trie.  
  * An audit of this code would be very valuable.  
* **Observability:** Except for normal blockchain monitoring this is out-of-scope.  
* **Deployability / Supportability / Operations:** This design is constrained to libraries. Only once these are implemented in products will it impact this point.   
* **Legal**: Legal was not consulted, because it was deemed not necessary.

# Logging and Monitoring

Doing logging and monitoring while adhering to the Web3 ethos and respecting data sovereignty is tricky, and out-of-scope.

# Implementation Plan

1. Phase 1 — Establishing foundation; In parallel.  
   1. **Members trie library**. Standalone. Add/delete/update members and devices, delta computation and application, proof generation.  
   2. **On-chain component**. Genesis ceremony, root-hash update via proxy multisig, epoch advancement, on-chain verification helpers.  
   3. **Formal model**. Scope: the τ-window invariants, the members trie ↔ substrate synchronization contract, the transitive trust acceptance rules. Outputs feed back into Phases 3 & 5 as invariants the implementation must preserve.  
   4. **Local-first library qualification/selection**. Build small spikes against both Keyhive and p2panda to verify the three substitutions actually work: (a) ACL referencing stable IDs with trie lookup for keys, (b) X25519 rotation independent of long-term identity, (c) interception/resolver for membership-change operations. Decide. Document the decision and the disqualifying gaps.  
2. Phase 2 — Create a **proof-of-concept progressive web app** to demonstrate functionality members trie and on-chain functionality.  
3. Phase 3 — **Local-first library substitutions**. The five items from “[Key changes required](#key-changes-required)” section, each with the corresponding Scenarios entry as exit criteria:  
   1. **Stable identities \+ trie-lookup key resolution**: revocation scenario passes, identity-takeover attack provably blocked.  
   2. **Organisation pseudo-group**: org-level delegation works for both example use cases from the PRD.  
   3. **Trie as sole write authority**: library-native membership ops are unreachable from the API surface.  
   4. **CGKA triggers (trie-driven)**: device-removal scenario triggers correct rotation; org-level updates trigger org-key rotation.  
   5. **p2p connection policy**: gating and transitive-trust scenarios pass, including τ-window jitter.  
4. Phase 4 — Create a **proof-of-concept progressive web app** to demonstrate functionality of creating a data object (e.g. text document) and sharing/revoking access rights.  
5. Phase 5 — **Integration & hardening**. End-to-end flows (onboarding, multi-trie-change-while-offline, all-devices-stolen edge case), SKU window minimization, optional org policies (offline-timeout, distrust-on-change). Debug flags. Audit.

# Addendum

## Keyhive vs p2panda comparison

* Both sets of libraries are implemented in Rust and can target WASM.  
* Both sets of libraries are pre-alpha (i.e. pre 1.0), explicitly disclaim production use, and have not been audited.   
* (Im)maturity:  
  * The Ink & Switch crates are pre-alpha (pre-1.0), explicitly disclaim production use, and have not been audited.  
    * BUT Ink & Switch’s CRDTs (Automerge) API seem to have stabilised, and the javascript library is at [version 3](https://automerge.org/blog/automerge-3/).  
  * On the p2panda side, p2panda-core/-net/-sync/-auth are released and iterating, p2panda-encryption has an audit scheduled with Radically Open Security, and p2panda-spaces — the integration crate ODS would depend on most — is still on a feature branch.   
  * Either choice likely requires vendoring specific revisions, contributing upstream fixes, and an independent security audit before production launch.  
* p2panda has a better separation of functionality, i.e. splitting out into libraries.  
* Ink & Switch’s CGKA (BeeKEM part of Keyhive) scales O(log n) per data object, whereas p2panda’s implementation only scales O(n).  
  * This is less of an issue than it seems at first glance, because of our delegation to the organisation-as-a-pseudo-group implementation, which keeps n (the number of groups to which access is delegated) small, at least a lot smaller than the organisation size, and the bigger the organisation the bigger the computation win.  
* Ink & Switch’s Keyhive sacrifices forward secrecy to be able to implement relay nodes. However, in the underlying BeeKEM functionality (not split out in a separate library) forward secrecy is easy to realise.  
* FYI: Ink & Switch’s Keyhive supports capability delegation by any holder to anyone (convergent capabilities). p2panda’s p2panda-auth is an ACL CRDT: only Manage-level members can mutate, i.e. delegation by non-managers is structurally disallowed. In this we have no strong preference.

## Scenarios (more detailed analysis)

For simplification we will consider most of the scenarios below at a person level, which is equivalent to each person only having 1 device each. Extending this to multiple devices per person is trivial, but would clutter the scenarios considered.

### Revocation: covers both device theft and person being fired / leaving {#revocation:-covers-both-device-theft-and-person-being-fired-/-leaving}

The/a device(s) of B learn they are no longer members of the organisation because they 

1. detected a change in the on-chain root hash,  
2. get the members trie delta,  
   1. (BUT, not the new organization secret key),  
3. confirm that they got the correct information by comparing with the on-chain root hash, and  
4. self deletes all organisation data on the affected device(s) of the member.

This “delete all device data” action will be true in all scenarios (below) where the device(s) of B learn they are no longer members of the organisation. This way it is possible to cover both device theft/lost and person B being fired / leaving.

### Gating: While A & B collaborates, B gets fired {#gating:-while-a-&-b-collaborates,-b-gets-fired}

Now consider the simple scenario where person B gets fired while collaborating with person A:  
![][image2]

After starting an active collaboration, both person A’s device and B’s device continue to regularly check against the blockchain whether the on-chain information has changed. The window between these checks are defined as 𝝉. Where 𝝉 is back-to-back, and stops and starts when the member’s device learns that either

1. there has been no change in the on-chain organisation root hash, since they last time they checked, or   
2. the members trie delta and new organisation secret key from peers (which they verify against the on-chain values).

Note that 𝝉 does NOT stop and start when the member’s device learns that the on-chain root hash has changed (instead the 𝝉 window gets extended till the member’s device has learned the members trie delta). 

If no membership change happens, e.g. during the transition from 𝝉Ax to 𝝉Ax+1 nothing happens. 

If A’s device learns that organisation membership has changed, due to a change in the on-chain members Merkle root hash change (as in 𝝉Ax+2), then A queries an organisation admin (or another member) for the Merkle delta (along with the new organisation wide secret key). In this case (after confirming the new calculated root hash) A learns that B is no longer a member, and immediately stops collaborating with B’s devices.

B’s device learns at the end of 𝝉Bx+2 their membership has been revoked and deletes all local data as per section “[Revocation: covers both device theft and person being fired / leaving](#revocation:-covers-both-device-theft-and-person-being-fired-/-leaving)”.

Note that during 𝝉Ax+2 collaboration with B isn’t interrupted, at least not until A has had the members trie update, i.e. the system defaults to availability for a better UX. The assumptions here are that:

1. A healthy organisation has low member turnover, and hence we optimise to interrupt the continued members’ work as little as possible. (That said, because this design extends local-first software, it could be implemented the other way around, where all that would happen is that the other parties would be experienced as offline during the latter part of 𝝉Ax+2 i.e. as soon as A learns of a new root hash, and until it has established whether B’s device is still in good standing as per the organisation definition.)  
2. The window 𝝉Ax+2 is “short”, i.e. A prioritises getting the added/delete/updated members/devices list describing the membership changes once learning of the root hash change.  
   1. If this takes long, there is merely a potentially larger piece of data discarded, i.e. the risk of a suboptimal UX.  
   2. If A goes offline before determining the new Merkle delta then 𝝉Ax+2 also effectively ends because it is no longer collaborating.

### Transitive trust: A & C window jitter (while B gets fired)

The astute reader would’ve noticed/realised that the 𝝉 windows of the different people/devices aren’t perfectly aligned. This is unavoidable in distributed systems, but acceptable as an initial implementation under the [transitive trust assumptions](#bookmark=id.8yt8biv4q2z6).

![][image3]

This is in essence the same example as section “[Gating: While A & B collaborates, B gets fired](#gating:-while-a-&-b-collaborates,-b-gets-fired)”, but here the windows 𝝉Ax+2 and 𝝉Cx+2, i.e. during which A’s device and C’s device learns that B is no longer a member, do not share the exact timing. This isn’t a problem, because of CRDT convergence and transitive trust. Specifically there is period of time during the latter part of  𝝉Cx+2 where A is no longer willing to accept changes from B (i.e. no peer-to-peer connection is possible), but C still does. These changes are then communicated to A by C, and A trusts changes that arrived inside a chunk signed by C's device, regardless of who authored them (since C in turn trusted at that point who they received chunks from), this is a standard aspect/ability of CRDT convergence. From 𝝉Ax+3 and 𝝉Cx+3 neither A nor C respectively will accept any connections/changes from B.

### Minimising “secret key update” (SKU) window ![][image4]

After updating their local members trie, there exists a (SKU) window in which the collaborators need to update their shared secret key for the data object they are collaborating on (i.e. their BeeKEM update). During this window they are effectively offline/disconnected from each other, hence it should be minimised:

1. Actively collaborating partners inform each other of the root hash changes (in advance of knowing the exact changes to the members trie), e.g. A immediately informs C:   
   1. This allows less regular checking of the root hash (avoid organisations DoS-ing the chain because all the devices of all member check the chain every second),   
   2. but minimise the time based information/context difference between actively collaborating partners.  
2. Similarly, the first device to learn the added/delete/updated members/devices list and new organisation secret key, informs their other active collaborating partners ASAP (assuming they weren’t one of the removed members/devices).  
   1. This decreases the likelihood of the admins being DoS’ed.  
   2. The party(s) who learned the added/delete/updated members/devices list and calculated the new Merkle delta can confirm it against the on-chain root hash (they’ve already previously learned in step 1 above).  
   3. The party(s) who learned the organisation secret key use a breakdown in communication with other members in good standing as an indicator that they might have the wrong secret key and confirm it with an admin.  
3. Now:  
   1. If the data object is shared organisation wide, they can switch to encryption using the new organisation secret key. I.e. the downtime should be almost imperceptible.  
   2. Otherwise, if there are no changes in the group of members nor their devices who have access, they can continue using the existing secret key (as determined by the local-first access control mechanism). I.e. the downtime should be almost imperceptible.  
   3. However, if the data object isn’t shared at organisation level, and the members/devices who have access to data object changed, then a new secret key (for said data object) needs to be calculated using the local-first access control methodology. This calculation might be delayed/slow if some relevant members are offline, the following mitigations are proposed:  
      1. If the actively collaborating members have existing common shared secret keys, then the highest common shared secret they have access to is used till a new shared secret can be calculated.  
      2. If the actively collaborating members do NOT have an existing common shared secret key, the secrets trie will be reshuffled to the maximum/biggest sub-trie that can be calculated by the actively collaborating members. (Reconstructing the complete secrets trie can be done asynchronously as the other members who have access to the data object come online.)

Note: The SKU does not impact 𝝉 window, i.e. the 𝝉 windows continue to be created back-to-back.

### Updating members trie

To avoid a malicious admin taking over an organisation, updates to the on-chain root hash require signatures from a threshold of admins. This threshold can be defined on a per organisation level, and adjusted over time.  
To keep the implementation as simple as possible we will be using a standard proxy multisig in this design. This is suboptimal because

1. It violates data sovereignty, because the public keys of the organisation admins are known / can be identified.  
2. It requires organisation admins to be managed separately from the organisation members trie, with the risk that the two can go out-of-sync.

In future work we might look at FROST or similar algorithms combined with smart contracts, to avoid the dual definition of admins independent of the organisation members trie.

### Revocation: Device stolen ⇐ per member devices sub-trie

The sole reason that the organisation members trie goes down to the member’s device resolution, because deleting a member’s device from the members trie allows the verifiable communication to a device to delete all its local data that belongs to the organisation. If we could find an alternative mechanism where users could manage their own devices that might be preferable for larger scale organisations.

**This does leave us with a multi-tier relationship between members and devices. Where members will address other members using their handles (e.g. define access control, send a message, etc) and encryption keys will be determined at a device level.**

### Onboarding new member/device {#onboarding-new-member/device}

It is envisaged that adding new members happens based on a secure off-chain exchange where the member discloses their preferred handle, their member public key, and their devices’ public keys. The admins mint the member’s `id` as part of the trie update; this `id` is stable for the lifetime of the member.

After the admins have updated the on-chain root, the new member can then get the whole members trie in addition to the organisation secret key from an admin or peer. Then they confirm it against the on-chain information.

### Multiple members trie changes while person is offline

If a member has been offline so long that multiple on-chain updates have happened, then the available members trie delta won’t be useful (because their trie is too old). Instead they use their public keys and that of a known peer to establish a secure channel where they can get the whole members trie along with the organisation secret, which they confirm against the on-chain information. (Similarly to a new onboarding.)

### Genesis ceremony

There needs to be a genesis “ceremony” smart contract, i.e. the starting definition of an organisation on-chain , wherewith

1. the proxy and multi-sig can be defined, and  
2. a smart contract that loads the first root hash and public key, and where public epoch \= 0\.

It should be possible to re-use the existing smart contract for updating the on-chain for this, with the constraint that the new\_epoch has to be zero (and no preceding root\_hash defined nor public\_key defined). Detailed mechanics of the genesis ceremony out of scope.

### Edge case: Member had all devices stolen

If a member has all their devices stolen, admins publish a trie update that rotates the member's public key to a fresh value, replaces devices sub-trie with a placeholder pending re-enrolment, and triggers the normal devices self delete, CGKA updates, and peer-to-peer connection checks — invalidating the stolen devices' access immediately. Because member’s id is stable and access control is delegated at that resolution, no data object access needs to be re-issued. Once the member has new devices, the standard onboarding flow (see [Onboarding new member/device](#onboarding-new-member/device)) updates their devices sub-trie with the new device keys.

[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image2]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image3]: <data:image/png;base64,>
